For the record: this appears to be a bug in `KqueueSelector`: when it calls `kqueue.control`, it sets `maxevents` (the maximum number of events to be returned, determines an internal buffer size) to match the number of fds registered with the selector. If there are no fds, it passes 0. The value 0 is special-cased by the `kevent` syscall, causing it to return immediately. (I can't imagine why anyone thought this was useful, but it is documented: ["When `nevents` is zero, `kevent()` will return immediately even if there is a `timeout` specified..."](https://www.freebsd.org/cgi/man.cgi?query=kqueue&sektion=2).) The real fix would be for `KqueueSelector` to add a line like `if max_ev == 0: max_ev = 1`. In the mean time of course curio has to work around it...

Also for the record, `SelectSelector` on Windows has a similar bug, which is discussed in #75.

Just wanna give a straight-forward feeling about the two kinds of conventions, I paste some example code as follows.

`Google Python Style`
```python
def fetch_bigtable_rows(big_table, keys, other_silly_variable=None):
    """Fetches rows from a Bigtable.

    Retrieves rows pertaining to the given keys from the Table instance
    represented by big_table.  Silly things may happen if
    other_silly_variable is not None.

    Args:
        big_table: An open Bigtable Table instance.
        keys: A sequence of strings representing the key of each table row
            to fetch.
        other_silly_variable: Another optional variable, that has a much
            longer name than the other args, and which does nothing.

    Returns:
        A dict mapping keys to the corresponding table row data
        fetched. Each row is represented as a tuple of strings. For
        example:

        {'Serak': ('Rigel VII', 'Preparer'),
         'Zim': ('Irk', 'Invader'),
         'Lrrr': ('Omicron Persei 8', 'Emperor')}

        If a key from the keys argument is missing from the dictionary,
        then that row was not found in the table.

    Raises:
        IOError: An error occurred accessing the bigtable.Table object.
    """
    pass
```

`Numpy Style`
``` python
def foo(var1, var2, long_var_name='hi') :
    r"""A one-line summary that does not use variable names or the
    function name.
    Several sentences providing an extended description. Refer to
    variables using back-ticks, e.g. `var`.
    Parameters
    ----------
    var1 : array_like
        Array_like means all those objects -- lists, nested lists, etc. --
        that can be converted to an array.  We can also refer to
        variables like `var1`.
    var2 : int
        The type above can either refer to an actual Python type
        (e.g. ``int``), or describe the type of the variable in more
        detail, e.g. ``(N,) ndarray`` or ``array_like``.
    Long_variable_name : {'hi', 'ho'}, optional
        Choices in brackets, default first when optional.
    Returns
    -------
    type
        Explanation of anonymous return value of type ``type``.
    describe : type
        Explanation of return value named `describe`.
    out : type
        Explanation of `out`.
    Other Parameters
    ----------------
    only_seldom_used_keywords : type
        Explanation
    common_parameters_listed_above : type
        Explanation
    Raises
    ------
    BadException
        Because you shouldn't have done that.
    See Also
    --------
    otherfunc : relationship (optional)
    newfunc : Relationship (optional), which could be fairly long, in which
              case the line wraps here.
    thirdfunc, fourthfunc, fifthfunc
    Notes
    -----
    Notes about the implementation algorithm (if needed).
    This can have multiple paragraphs.
    You may include some math:
    .. math:: X(e^{j\omega } ) = x(n)e^{ - j\omega n}
    And even use a greek symbol like :math:`omega` inline.
    References
    ----------
    Cite the relevant literature, e.g. [1]_.  You may also cite these
    references in the notes section above.
    .. [1] O. McNoleg, "The integration of GIS, remote sensing,
       expert systems and adaptive co-kriging for environmental habitat
       modelling of the Highland Haggis using object-oriented, fuzzy-logic
       and neural-network techniques," Computers & Geosciences, vol. 22,
       pp. 585-588, 1996.
    Examples
    --------
    These are written in doctest format, and should illustrate how to
    use the function.
    >>> a=[1,2,3]
    >>> print [x + 3 for x in a]
    [4, 5, 6]
    >>> print "a\n\nb"
    a
    b
    """

    pass
```

The problem with this is that now the software that's calling this is now dependent on the output of the SVN command. This project is meant to be an adapter/abstraction of Subversion. The whole point is to prevent the caller from getting involved in Subversion internals.

```
>>> from ldap3.utils.dn import escape_rdn_chars
>>> a = 'baduser,bad=stuff'
>>> print(escape_rdn_chars(a))  # correct
'baduser\\,bad\\=stuff'
>>> b = 'cn='+a+',ou=users,o=test'
>>> print(escape_rdn_chars(b))  # wrong
>>> 'cn\\=baduser\\,bad\\=stuff\\,ou\\=utenti\\,o\\=test'
```

Well, I'm not totally sure if it does, but for now it looks promising. More testing might reveal some more hidden issues.
At least, we were now able to have it restart and append the new information to the original database file.

However, after the optimisation process itself crashes and gets restarted, new data will appended regardless of the actual progress. Thus, the user may end up having partly completed generations within the database that get expanded by the restarted generation. So, some generations might end up being "streched" in terms of produced results, or number of lines in the database (I hope that explaination is kind of understandable...). We use another script to initiate the restart and let it write markers into the database, such that we can keep track of the completed generations and remove the partly completed ones. Maybe it might be interesting to consider this to become baseline spotpy functionality, that a restart will automatically write markers.
Furthermore, specifically for the SCEUA, it might be interesting to provide further means of process termination. For now, if I understand it correctly, spotpy (with SCEUA) can terminate because it reached the threshold defining convergence, exhaust the amount of trails allowed or being stopped forcefully/crash. 
We think it would be good if the user could provide spotpy with another parameter. This would be the amount of generations, again focused on the SCEUA, it shall complete during a single run. Afterwards it would stop gracefully, writing the results to the db, create a breakpoint for restart and terminate. Maybe it could even check for the existance of a specific *.stop file, or something, to trigger the graceful stop at the next suitable opportunity. Of course, the user could restrict the number of trails allowed and expand this amount for each restart, however it would be nicer as proper functionality.
This behaviour would be very useful, when utilising computing resources employing a scheduler and "computing time tokens", for more efficiently using the computing time. 


We have discovered further issues, that seem to arise somewhere in the mix of spotpy, our script framework that we use to control spotpy functionality and the comutational resources we have access to. This makes it a bit tricky to figure out what is what, there. It might be better to discuss this in seperate issues, if actually related to spotpy.
But yeah, either way there should be a consistent API in oscrypto for disabling or enabling revocation checks. And then, that would need to be expose to/via the async API.

You can get select-like behaviour just by using the `asyncio` stdlib. `wait` is your friend here.

But a nice unix-y `select()` would be useful.

Using the approach from the linked page is probably the wrong way to go about it though.
Threading-based channels have issues that we don't need to think about.
A select for `Channel` (which in fact would work for any list of futures) would be much much simpler.

I think it would be fairly easy to build on top of `asyncio.wait()`.
I'm not a 100% sure on how to handle channels closing,
and if using wait and then cancelling the `pending` ones when a `done` future is returned, or
if it would make more sense to let the select be a more specialised class that holds state and is only for Channels. 

One option would be to do something like:
```python

from aiochannel import aioselect, Channel, ChannelClosed

c1 = Channel()
c2 = Channel()
c3 = Channel()
# ...
async def main():
    channels = [c1, c2, c3]
    while len(channels) > 0:
        try:
            (channel, value) = await aioselect(channels, timeout=5.0)
        except ChannelClosed:
            # one of the channels have been closed
            channels = filter(lambda c: not c.is_closed(), channels)
        do_work(channel, value)
    # all channels closed here (or timeout)
```

alternatively, a `Channel`-only select could handle close events internally
```python

from aiochannel import Channel, ChannelClosed

c1 = Channel()
c2 = Channel()
c3 = Channel()

# ...

async def main():
    channels = [c1, c2, c3]
    # The timeout is for the entire block
    async with Channel.select(channels, timeout=5.0) as selector:
        while not selector.closed(): # a selector will close when all channels are closed
            (channel, value) = await selector(timeout=1.0) # timeout for individual items
            do_work(channel, value)
    # all channels are closed here (or timeout)
```

Both of the above examples should probably thow a `TimeoutError` on timeout.

The first example doesn't really save much code and is error-prone. It will probably also perform worse then example two, where we can hide a bunch of cleverness in the async context manager.

So I think the second approach would be a good candidate.
Thoughts?

While on sane networks, you should be able to get along with:

* packet timeout between 1 and 5 minutes (for authentication, ping etc) 
* ping the server after 15 or 45 minutes without incoming packets (either 0198 ack request or 0199 ping)
* ping / 0198 resume on connectivity change

I think that adaptive packet timeouts are an interesting thing, but in the general case you'll get a response within 60s. Latency spikes are typically outliers where you can do a 0198 resume afterwards. 

Most NATs come with acceptable timeouts, typically 60m. If you are on a broken network, you could attempt to work around it with adaptive ping intervals, but this is really a tiny minority. 

If you send a ping after a connection change,  your stack should be smart enough to close the connection. Then you can resume immediately.

> because "too much magic"

I agree :) It was started as a proof-of-concept, but people use it, so I try to keep it as stable as it is possible. And as the time passes, there is some empiric accumulated about different kernel flavours and their behaviour — the RTNL API in the kernel is quite messy. I can not put much of this empiric into the `IPRoute` interface, since it's supposed to be more or less a pure RTNL reflection. But it stays in the `commit()` call in IPDB plugins.

> Does the entire configuration is rollbacked or only the modifications that have been applied?

The algo is as follows:
1. on the first interface change, IPDB makes a «linked snapshot» of the interface, that keeps all the changes made from this IPDB, but also reflects «3rd party» changes — IPDB listens for RTNL updates continuously.
2. on `commit()`, the interface IPDB plugin (route and rule plugins behave a bit different) makes two additional «interface» objects:
```python
removed, added = snapshot // transaction
# roughly, it's equal to:
added = transaction - snapshot
removed = snapshot - transaction
```
3. upon failure, IPDB runs `commit()` with `spanshot` as the transaction. It must **revert only those changes, that are made by the failed `commit()`**. But since RTNL has no locking, theoretically there may be some races.

Thus,
> suppose that before commiting a change, an unrelated interface is modified by a program (an IP address is added to it for example). Is there a risk that the change is cancelled if my own change fails for some reason?

No, should not be. If it happens, it is a bug and should be fixed. By design IPDB is supposed to run as a **non**-exclusive settings DB, synced with the real system state, whatever can change it at any time.

That's why there is still no support for the traffic controls in the IPDB — there is no changes broadcasting in the kernel TC subsystem. But interfaces, addresses, neighbours, routes and rules are synced just fine.

```python
from typing import Union

@dataclass

Hi @loh-maa, I always recommend checking out the README when changes like this are made, I try to keep it updated. 

If we take a look at the Getting Started section you'll see some information on using a service account, the most important part of which is "Adding a service account will authenticate as an admin by default for all database queries". So once we add a service account we don't need to use any other auth methods, this is all handled automatically, for example:

```
import pyrebase

config = {
  "apiKey": "apiKey",
  "authDomain": "projectId.firebaseapp.com",
  "databaseURL": "https://databaseName.firebaseio.com",
  "storageBucket": "projectId.appspot.com",
  "serviceAccount": "path/to/serviceAccountCredentials.json"
}

firebase = pyrebase.initialize_app(config)
db = firebase.database()
db.child("users").push({"name": "henry"})
```
If the service account credential is [set up properly](https://firebase.google.com/docs/server/setup#add_firebase_to_your_app) this should authenticate you as an admin and allow you to read/write to Firebase.

Tornado-JSON actually doesn't adhere to JSON API; my reason for doing this was because I wanted to Tornado-JSON to be simple and not bogged down by the significant complexity of a spec like JSON API, which, let's admit is gigantic.

Currently, Tornado-JSON follows [JSend](http://labs.omniti.com/labs/jsend) (as is said in the README) because the spec is simple and straightforward and let's you just get on with building small APIs. I am not opposed to following JSON API in the future, but some significant work would have to go into it before that happens.

`def StartStream(self, body, xcloud_id):

		body['from'] = self.user_id+'_web'

It very much depends on the purpose and hence what kind of properties you want to ensure. If you limit yourself to endpoints that can be represented without loss of precision in computer arithmetics (i.e., integers and rationals) I would probably keep an additional flag for each endpoint to store whether it is included in the interval.

If you need more general endpoints, i.e., if you want to model intervals with endpoints in the set of real numbers, soon or later you'll end up with either a set of guaranteed properties too weak to be useful or with “wrong” results, i.e., in situations where your results won't satisfy the properties that you initially set up to guarantee.

ade1451a735619f6fd569b08239028f6220101b1 resolves the issue of disordered results. The function "physical_values_ordered_by_class" returns a list of physical values ordered by class name and attribute name. Note that if two PriorModels share a Prior then the length of the physical_values vector will be longer than the input unit_vector because one resulting value will be returned twice, once for each PriorModel with which the share Prior is associated.

These changes do not mean that the input unit hypercube vector retains the same order. When a new prior is created then the last value in the hypercube vector will be passed to that prior. Any prior creation should happen before multinest is used in conjunction with the priors module such that the length and ordering of the required unit hypercube vector never changes.

Yeah there is quite a bit of dead code in coins.py; I will remove it.  A lot of it originates from when it was intended for a wallet implementation...  though some of the code you highlight was used in older versions of ElectrumX that indexed by address.  Now it indexes by scripthash, as that is independent of address format (e.g. BCH's cashaddress; also BTC's segwit lark).

We add to the DB not the hash of the PK but the hash of the script, as the function name indicates.  ElectrumX indexes by (hash of) the script, and Electrum subscribes to the (hashes of) P2PKH scripts only by default.  You just need to adjust Electrum to subscribe to P2PK scripts and ElectrumX will return the data and you would see it in Electrum.  Note that changing Electrum is harder than it sounds, as it only signs P2PKH inputs, so you'd need to extend its signature scheme too to be able to spend them.

@cavaunpeu, it`s been a while so it might not be an issue anymore for you but to keep as a reference to others, this is how I run my app with flask-webpack without the production server:

My project structure looks like this:

    app_name 
      | app_name
          | build
          __init__.py


In my `__init__.py` file i have:

```python
import os
from flask import Flask, render_template
from flask_webpack import Webpack

app = Flask(
    __name__,
    static_folder=os.path.join(os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__)), "build"),
    static_url_path="/static"
)

app.config["WEBPACK_ASSETS_URL"] = "/static/"
app.config["WEBPACK_MANIFEST_PATH"] = os.environ["WEBPACK_MANIFEST_PATH"]

webpack = Webpack()
webpack.init_app(app)


@app.route("/")
def index():
    return render_template("home.jinja2")
```
`static_folder` sets where flask looks for static files. Set it to your webpack build output.
`static_url_path` sets the url prefix flask will listen(it is a route in flask) to serve the `static_folder` assets. In this case the resulting path will be`example.com/static/app.hash.css`.

Check the [ Flask API docs for more](http://flask.pocoo.org/docs/0.11/api/).

So it follows that `static_url_path` must be the same as `publicPath` in webpack.config.js and `WEBPACK_ASSETS_URL`.

The bind that I was in (which I've now solved in a different way that doesn't require proxies or monkey patching, so I'm happier) is that altering my `original` object has too *long* an effect, but using `with` is too *short*.

It's a strange situation, but basically a class `A` has a callback routine `create_c()` that produces instances of class `C`, for further processing by the caller of this `create_c` method.  I wanted to make a derived subclass of `A` that overrides `create_c` to get the parent's `create_c` output, then modify its `inner()` behavior.  This modification needed to persist as long as the caller used it, but (here's the trick) *not* for the rest of the lifespan of the underlying object, which may be used again in other contexts and needs to have its original `inner()` semantics in those contexts.  And since `create_c` *returns* the `C` object to its caller, where the new semantics need to hold, it's not possible to use a `with` context manager.

If that description isn't clear, I can try to code up a little example.

If I weren't in architectural control of some of the callers here, and couldn't refactor things, then I'd be in a tough spot and I'd really need to get this proxying to work the way I'd originally intended.  

Seems like it could be a useful technique if you do find time to add it to the examples!  Essentially it would give people "on-the-fly subclassing" of individual instances of a class, for whatever purpose they felt like using it for.

Ok, I now understand how it gets there. So, this means, in your audit log, you have entries that actually point at a specific translation instead of the generic model.

This is indeed something that's not supported, because the main concept of `hvad` is it does not actually appear in the database schema, but acts as a drop-in replacement for field values. It supports a “display language”, which has no explicity identity. This is the reason creating foreign keys to the translations model is not supported, and the translations model is mostly hidden as an implementation detail.

Now, it is true that when you use **hvad** in a project and you have a single instance where you want to interact with translations, it can make sense to “misuse” **hvad** a bit. Usually, when possible, I would recommend doing that by:
  - keying to the main model (by putting the `AuditMixin` on the main model)
  - and adding a `language_code` field to the audit entries.

This does have the drawback of requiring the audit model to be aware of the language. I understand how this can be problematic in some cases.

=====

Anyway, I traced your issue, it comes from the optimizations that were added in the latest version of **hvad**:
  - The `update_fields` parameter is now supported in `TranslatableModel.save`. It used to be just passed around for `django` to handle it, but now `hvad` can detect when only translated or only untranslated fields are being saved, and avoid updating the two tables when that happens.
- The serializer now builds and passes a list of fields to `update_fields` when it updates translations.

This dramatically cuts down on the number of SQL requests: before, if you updated 5 translations, 10 SQL requests were sent (the untranslatable fields were updated 5 times). Now, only 6 requests are sent (1 for untranslatable fields and 1 per translation).

As translation models cannot normally have m2m fields, the `update_translation` method does not filter them out. Thus your problem. Should not be too hard to fix, and will improve compatibility with some of the edge case hvad is put to use in. It needs to update all fields, but only pass regular fields to `update_fields`.

A proper fix will require building a sample testcase with failing test, which might actually be trickier than the fix itself. I cannot do it right now, but I should be able to spend some time on this in the weekend.

@cacheback(lifetime=10)
def what_time_is_it():
    return datetime.utcnow()

def the_view(request):
    actually_now = datetime.utcnow()
    return HttpResponse(repr([actually_now, what_time_is_it(), actually_now - what_time_is_it()],
                        content_type='text/plain')
```

What you'll see in this case is that the initial call does cache the results, but subsequent calls will continue to use the originally cached results forever.  You won't see any errors coming from the celery tasks, because they are successfully updating the cache at the wrong key.

> why is this not the default?

Because it can cause multiple event handlers for a given client to run in parallel. In same cases this is fine, but in some others it may not, and it may require the application to explicitly protect against race conditions due to the parallelism.

In general, it is not a great idea to have event handlers that are slow. It would be better to start a secondary background task that sleeps for one second and then emits than having that in the event handler function. If you code the sleep and the second emit in a background task, then you should not need to use the async_handlers option.

Meh, I think it conformed to specs. What good is software that doesn't conform to specs 😉 ?  I never wanted to support every emoji image set out there like Phantom etc.  I guess I could have provided an option to feed in your own dictionaries of links, but that is half the extension.  At that point, you might as well just fork the original and do what you want.  And like you mentioned, it is easy enough for people to fork the code and have it support the image set of their choice if they like, even their own local set if desired.  Github made it very easy to poll their emoji and get the links to their images for free, so why not.

With that said, expanding it's specs to output the raw Unicode isn't a bad option, and I think I've generally warmed up to this.  You get Github's syntax, but you also get something you can use without Github's image CDN.  I think this new raw output is very reasonable and flexible and now more general.  And if a specific, different image set is desired instead, fork it :smile:.

I'm afraid you miss the point.
The failing code works fine being run using ordinary `.backward()` call:

```
import mxnet as mx
from mxnet import nd, autograd

ctx = mx.cpu()

a = mx.nd.array([1, 2, 3, 4], ctx=ctx)
a.attach_grad()

with autograd.record():
    b = nd.sum(2 * (a[0:4] ** 2))

b.backward()
print(a.grad)
```

Why does it fail when I retain the graph by using `autograd.grad` and then re-use this graph again directly via `.bind`? And such re-use fails *only* when the slicing is used. I deliberately made slicing `a[0:4]` because it doesn't change the shape of the expression and technically should be equal to just `a`. Without slicing both `autograd.grad` and `.bind` do their jobs perfectly well.

`reset_db` connects to the (`PostgreSQL`) database on `template1` instead of your Django database name because it wants to issue `DROP DATABASE ...` and `CREATE DATABASE ...` commands which it cannot do when connected to the database used for Django. (`template1` exists by default in PG)

This basically means that your user (named `USER`) needs to be able to access the `template1` database and at least have the rights to drop that database and create a new one. (or be a superuser)

You can also use the `--user` and friends command line option to change the user `reset_db` uses for `PostgreSQL` from the one specified in `settings.py` to whatever the correct user is.

Right now there is no command line option to connect to another database then `template1`. I'm happy to accept a patch for that.

Hope this helps.

I really don't know how Riak or Yokozuna works internally, but what I believe is happening is that, when I delete the index, Riak mark is as deleted instantly, so it returns "Index not found" exceptions regardless the index is present or not in Solr. So what I did is to bypass Riak and talk to Solr directly:

```python
def wait_for_delete_index():
    while True:
        try:
            urllib2.urlopen('http://localhost:8093/internal_solr/my_index/select')
            # Schema found in Solr, try again
            time.sleep(1)
        except urllib2.HTTPError:
            # Schema not found in Solr
            return

c.delete_search_index('my_index')

About signed vs. unsigned: signed becomes practical in implementations, where you want to iterate backwards. Since that is algorithmic question and has nothing to do with the libraries interface, I would keep the unsigned values since they express explicitly that you are dealing with sizes and indices. I think this is a programming style issue. I grabbed this mindset from you and expanded upon it. I actually like it. For example, only the ```/certificates``` API call uses ```certtoken``` today; but that may change.
```
$ egrep _add_with_cert_auth CloudFlare/api_v4.py
            self._add_with_cert_auth(self._base, "certificates"))
$ 
```
 The ```_add_with_cert_auth``` ends up using ```call_with_certauth``` which is the only place in the codebase that needs to check ```certtoken```.
```
        def call_with_certauth(self, method,
            ...
            if self.certtoken is '':
                raise CloudFlareAPIError(0, 'no cert token defined')
            headers = {
                'X-Auth-User-Service-Key': self.certtoken,
                'Content-Type': 'application/json'
            }
            ...
```
I think this is as efficient as is needed.

Going back to your initial PR and the change you requested. Note that the code for ```certtoken``` checking is in the class init function. This simply makes this option in-line with account info. It's normally allocated on a per-account basis. This seems the right way to implement this.

Hi. I was hitting the same error and came up with this solution. If you're okay with rounding, you may find it helpful. 
```
def round_float_to_decimal(float_value):
    """
    Convert a floating point value to a decimal that DynamoDB can store,
    and allow rounding.
    """

    # Perform the conversion using a copy of the decimal context that boto3
    # uses. Doing so causes this routine to preserve as much precision as
    # boto3 will allow.
    with decimal.localcontext(boto3.dynamodb.types.DYNAMODB_CONTEXT) as \
         decimalcontext:

        # Allow rounding.
        decimalcontext.traps[decimal.Inexact] = 0
        decimalcontext.traps[decimal.Rounded] = 0
        decimal_value = decimalcontext.create_decimal_from_float(float_value)
        g_logger.debug("float: {}, decimal: {}".format(float_value,
                                                       decimal_value))

        return decimal_value
```

The problem with attaching to a existing session include:
- Robot Framework variables initiated up to the point of failure based on the things that vary at runtime will not have useful values in the second run
- You have disable the teardown to prevent the session from being destroyed.
- For Grid, you would have to execute the second run and attach to the session before it times out (2 minutes or so?)

The django-brambling repository is a project repository, not a reusable package. We use it to deploy our servers, so it's imperative that the Pipfile.lock is honored, even if we have git dependencies. In this case, our pipfile.lock correctly listed django 1.8.11 as the locked version, but django 1.11.12 was installed despite that.

More generally, I would also expect that explicitly stating a requirement for a particular version of a package would have precedence over all subdependencies of packages I might list, no matter what order things are listed in.

Regarding the structuring suggestions:

My understanding of `install_requires` has always been that it's intended for open-source packages, to ensure that dependencies are automatically installed via pip, not for setting up a virtual environment with specific packages for a deploy; since this is for a project repo, I'm hesitant to use install_requires. Are you saying that install_requires is used by pipenv as a "base" for the requirements listed in the two package sections? That isn't something I've ever noticed in the documentation, but maybe I missed it?

The reason that I have tox and django-debug-toolbar in `[packages]` is because _in the past_ when I've put them in `[dev-packages]` and then installed dev packages, they've conflicted with the non-dev installed packages. For example, if I put django in `[packages]` at 1.8.11 and django-debug-toolbar in `[dev-packages]`, I end up with django 2.0 installed, as a dependency of django-debug-toolbar.

I feel [PEP 508]-style markers is the better approach to this kind of problems. Instead of just `mypy = "*"`, write this in the Pipfile:

```toml
mypy = { version = "*", platform_python_implementation = "=='CPython'" }
```

The syntax should be pretty straightforward, and there are quite some variants available. See [PEP 508] for a list of available markers, and examples in [Pipfile]’s README for some examples.

My opinion: Locale should be introduced when the differences in word forms are big enough to split detection into two separate languages. We're probably going to get there with Chinese (zh_CN and zh_TW), but for minor differences, like this one, I would vote for a variant within one language. We could use negative lookahead expressions like `(?!\.)` to make sure that's not an abbreviation.

> @scikit-image/core Do we want to introduce a new ridges submodule? My feeling is that we don't have enough potential filters to put in there, and that these should remain grouped with our edge detectors; thoughts welcome.

I think functionally ridge detection is distinct from edge detection and it is nice to have those routines grouped together in one file.  Is there really much downside to having a submodule vs. a private `_ridges.py`?  I don't have a strong preference either way. 

That said, these are all Hessian-based routines.  Related filters have been created for enhancement/detection of shapes  that aren't edges or ridges.  For examples of dot/blob detection, see:

Li Q, S. Sone, and K. Doi, “Selective enhancement filters for nodules, vessels, and airway walls in two- and three-dimensional CT scans,” Med. Phys., vol. 30, no. 8, pp. 2040–2051, 2003.

T. Jerman, F. Pernus, B. Likar, Z. Spiclin, "Blob Enhancement and Visualization for Improved Intracranial Aneurysm Detection", IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics, 22(6), p. 1705-1717 (2016), https://doi.org/10.1109/TVCG.2015.2446493)

Hm... it seems like you have a different expectation of what preserve_range
should do: rescaling the range of values vs. clipping. I don't think this
is what I would expect from "preserve_range=True" (emphasis on "I" as other
people might think differently of course) and what is currently implement
e.g. in the warping functions. I expect the following: if I pass in an
array of any type, the function does not mess with the range of values
simply because of data type conversion. E.g., there is no obvious reason
that a warping function rescales the range of values just because it has to
"up-cast" the values to float/double. However, this often happens right now
as our img_as_* functions rescale the range of values automatically. A
default value of "preserve_range=True" could solve this issue, even though
we need to be careful since some functions may actually rely on the
rescaling. Instead of implementing `preserve_range` on a package-wide using
a generic function, I think this must be done on a per-function basis to
apply this behavior to the appropriate input and output parameters.

This [line](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/2.7/Lib/httplib.py#L895) fails.

```Python
if isinstance(message_body, str):
            msg += message_body # here
```

 because ```self_buffer``` contains both ```unicode``` and ```str```.

```Python
>>> self._buffer
[u'POST /api/6/store/ HTTP/1.1',
 'Accept-Encoding: identity',
 u'Content-Length: 828',
 'Content-Encoding: deflate',
 u'Connection: close',
 'X-Sentry-Auth: Sentry sentry_timestamp=1473708698.35, sentry_client=raven-python/5.26.0, sentry_version=6, sentry_key=********************************, sentry_secret=********************************',
 'User-Agent: raven-python/5.26.0',
 u'Host: sentry.domain.com',
 'Content-Type: application/octet-stream',
 '',
 '']
```


And ```message_body``` contains bytes that can't be expressed in ```unicode```?

```message_body
'x\x9c\xadUm\x8b\xdc6\x10\xfe+\xc2\xf9\xe0;\xc8\xfa\xfdm\x97\xb4\xb4\xcd] .... '
```

The reason ```self._buffer``` contains both ```str``` and ```unicode``` is that with ```future``` urllib2 properties are ```unicode```.

`tee` is problematic because when utilities' output is piped through it, the standard output/error of the utilities is no longer a terminal. In turn, many of them don't switch to line buffered mode and use 4k-buffering, turn off producing colors, and interactive apps probably even refuse to start up (or start up in some degraded mode, e.g. no line editing).

`script` is a much better and more transparent approach for such logging.

The core reason why this story is technically problematic is because terminal emulators don't just expect to receive a continuous text that they print; there's a lot more to this. E.g. the cursor might be moved freely, existing text may be overwritten etc. Does one want to have a log of what "permanently" remains on the screen, or a log of everything that gets printed, even if possibly later overwritten? For the latter, again, `script` is the right tool.

For the former: The concept of something "permanently" remaining on the screen can't really be defined. Getting scrolled out of the default viewport is a good starting point, since scrolled out data can't really be modified. So the "log" could be composed of what has scrolled out (almost fixed data) + what's visible now on the screen (variable).

Note that stuff that has been scrolled out can still be modified if the window is enlarged so that this text comes back to the "active" area.

Going with this approach would probably require additional support from VTE.

For reference again, a great non-invasive way to profile the web API.

Gunicorn configution file updated from [mes-aides-ui/openfisca/api_config.py](https://github.com/sgmap/mes-aides-ui/blob/master/openfisca/api_config.py) (then use _snakeviz_ on generated profiling files, thanks @fpagnoux).

```python
port = 2000
workers = 1
timeout = 600

country_package = 'openfisca_france'
extensions = ['openfisca_paris', 'openfisca_brestmetropole', 'openfisca_rennesmetropole']

import cProfile
import pstats
import StringIO
import logging
import os
import time

def pre_request(worker, req):
    import cProfile
    import pstats
    import StringIO
    import logging
    import os
    import time
    worker.start_time = time.time()
    worker.profile = cProfile.Profile()
    worker.profile.enable()
    worker.log.info("PROFILING %d: %s" % (worker.pid, req.uri))

def post_request(worker, req, *args):
    import cProfile
    import tempfile
    tf = tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile(delete = False)
    print("http://127.0.0.1:8080/snakeviz/" + tf.name)
    worker.profile.dump_stats(tf.name)
```

Right, the key for `populate` is to specify which `ParameterCollection/Parameter/LookupParameter` should be loaded into.
1. If key is `""`, it assumes the whole file only contains one `ParameterCollection/Parameter/LookupParameter`. In other words, it will seek to the beginning of the file.

Something like this:

```
>>> import dill
>>>
>>> def foo(x,y):
...   return x+y
... 
>>> class Bar(object):
...   def zap(self):
...     return foo(self.x,self.x)
...   def __init__(self, x):
...     self.x = x
... 
>>> import dill
>>> 
>>> _foo = dill.source.importable(foo)
>>> _Bar = dill.source.importable(Bar)
>>>                               
>>> b = Bar(4)
>>> _b = dill.dumps(b, byref=True)
>>> 
>>> _b 
'\x80\x02c__main__\nBar\nq\x00)\x81q\x01}q\x02U\x01xq\x03K\x04sb.'
>>> 
>>> del b
>>> del foo
>>> del Bar
>>> 
>>> exec(_foo)
>>> exec(_Bar)
>>> b = dill.loads(_b)
>>> b.zap()
8
>>> 
```

You can see for instances, I'm still relying on `dill.dumps`.  I'm doing that because I know you want to go across versions of python.  Actually, `dill.source.importable` does work for instances of classes, but it doesn't do it by reference, which is probably what you want.

I did put a wrapper around `dill.source.importable` so it dumps to a temporary file.  That's `dill.temp.dump_source`, and it has am accompanying `dill.temp.load_source`.  The docs show exactly how to use it.  I'm not sure that is what you want.

There are a limited number of examples, however, you can check out `test_source.py` in the `tests` directory.  `dill.source` is used in `ppft`, which is like `multiprocessing`, but works with source code instead of serialized objects.

I hope that helps.

> Column has an attribute called handles_field_classes that is a list of
> the modelfields it can query for searches. (BooleanColumn lists
> models.BooleanField and models.NullBooleanField for example, because you
> query those the same way in the database.)
>
> Using the following example:
>
> class MyDatatable(Datatable):
>     class Meta:
>         model = BlogEntry
>         columns = ['id', 'name', 'is_published']
>
> At this stage, the column system doesn't know anything about is_published,
> so it looks up the field on the model. It discovers that it's a Django
> BooleanField, but it still needs to look up a Column class to handle
> BooleanFields. It iterates over the private column registry until it finds
> a Column class with a handles_field_classes list that contains the model
> field BooleanField.
>
> Normally, it iterates the registry until it finds the built-in
> BooleanColumn, but if you declare a new column class, it goes into the top
> of the registry with top priority, and will be found first.
>
> As a counter-example, this Datatable would respect your choice for
> BooleanColumn because you've provided it explicitly:
>
> class MyDatatable(Datatable):
>     is_published = columns.BooleanColumn("Published")
>
>     class Meta:
>         model = BlogEntry
>         columns = ['id', 'name', 'is_published']
>
> Hopefully that clarifies it!
>

```
import docx

def add_bookmark(document, bookmark_text, bookmark_name):
    document.add_paragraph().add_run()
    tag = document.element.xpath('//w:r')[-1]
    start = docx.oxml.shared.OxmlElement('w:bookmarkStart')
    start.set(docx.oxml.ns.qn('w:id'), '0')
    start.set(docx.oxml.ns.qn('w:name'), bookmark_name)
    tag.append(start)

    text = docx.oxml.OxmlElement('w:r')
    text.text = bookmark_text
    tag.append(text)
    end = docx.oxml.shared.OxmlElement('w:bookmarkEnd')
    end.set(docx.oxml.ns.qn('w:id'), '0')
    end.set(docx.oxml.ns.qn('w:name'), bookmark_name)
    tag.append(end)

```

Sorry, I missed the motivation behind this change in the previous discussion.

Adding support for every build system out there so people can embed snowball into their projects just isn't going to scale, even if there's someone willing to maintain each of them.  I think we should really be working towards making snowball more usable as an installed dependency, rather than making it easier for people to embed a copy of it in their projects.

If we have two build systems in the main repo, we're going to have to find out which people are using each time there's a report of a bug report where that might be relevant.  Sometimes they'll actually think to tell us, or it'll be obvious from the error, but experience tells me we'll have to ask a lot of the time.

So if you really want to be able to embed snowball as you are, I think it's more sensible to maintain that separately.  It's likely to be less work for you that way - e.g. you don't need to add support for the jsx or python backends (#24) if you aren't using them in rspamd.  It also means that your "snowball with cmake" tree can always be in a consistent state, which is more helpful to anyone else wanting to use it.  If cmake support is in the main repo, then it'll be out of step for a bit after any change to the makefiles.

Hi, a  generic version of what you propose would be very useful for me and many others. Many are seeking to integrate Sonos into an intelligent home by using it for announcements, door bell, etc. 
This is where the snapshot code came from, as a part step. 

I have tried several approaches with groups and I believe it can be done but there are some challenges. 
1) the time it takes to discover the current state of each zone, group for playing the alert can mount up. Code is very fast - even on a Raspberry Pi, its the network traffic time that mounts up depending on your network. I have currently opted not to group the zones, just to play an alert on each coordinator. Yes there can appear a bit of an echo but it works faster. 

2) If you leave some zones playing it may be that it's coordinator is required for the announcement, hence you have to pass off what is playing to the slave. Quite doable, just another complexity and more time. 

3) you have to group and ungroup in the right order or you can get errors.

I have tried making grouping efficient by looking for the biggest current group and then bringing other zones into that - this can speed up grouping a lot. 

The other approach I have tried it to have a Raspberry Pi running permanently, and subscribing to events from all Sonos zones. This means when an alert needs to be played I already know the current groups and what is playing on each zone. 

Looking forward - ideally we could get to something like:
```
mysonos.alert(zone_list, alert_stream, resume=No/Yes/Fade, other_zones=ignore, mute)
```
Just my thoughts and experiences. 
Happy to help create something - lets get others thoughts and ideas.

Cheers David

Thanks for merging these pieces!

There are two reasons why I ended up changing ExaBGP behavior wrt. __repr__ and __str__:
* some `__hash__`, `__eq__`, `__ne__` or `__cmp__` implementations actually rely on repr or str to expose the right things to behave properly wrt. to how bagpipe uses nlri, attributes and RTs in sets and dicts
* bagpipe logs use repr on some of these objects

The reason why I proposed to use repr instead of str is the following: str(iterable) calls repr on the internal objects, thus str(multivalue attribute) ends up calling repr on each attribute (e.g. for extended communities).  By replacing all the `__str__` definitions by `__repr__` definitions, I get the wanted behavior, *and* `__str__` still works because if __str__ is undefined but __repr__ is Python calls __repr__ (Python does not do it the reverse way: if __repr__ is undefined, repr(object) displays the object address, which is useless).

I would still like `__repr__` or `__str__` to return something useful, if you end up deciding that __str__ should be used, this would be also fine for bagpipe, but for extended communities I would need a tweak on bagpipe side so that str is called on members of attributes[Attribute.CODE.EXTENDED_COMMUNITY].communities.

Another thing is, if you drop __str__ and __repr__, bagpipe would need that `__hash__`, `__eq__`, `__ne__` and `__cmp__` behavior is made consistent to allow storing objects in sets and/or using them in hashes.  We may need a discussion focused on this particular issue because, the above pull request still does not get the right behavior for bagpipe:  
* example 1: hash and cmp defined in MPLSVPN are good, but cmp is in fact not called when MPLSVPN objects are used in sets because eq/neq defined in MPLS are defined and used preferably. I can override eq/neq in MPLSVPN
* example 2: using RTs as dict keys or in sets would not work because cmp defined in extended.Community is not called, because eq/neq defined in Attribute is preferred ; I can override eq/neq in ExtendedCommunity or in RouteTarget, but I fear that the behavior would not be consistent with the behavior of eq of Attribute (which tests only attribute id, but not the value); my question is: do the Attribute and Attributes classes depend on this behavior of Attribute.eq or could we change this ?

Hi,



I am not quite sure what you mean by show only print() and not stdout.write(). Aren't those two things essentially the same?

If by the .py script you are referring to the python script that you bind in your API to exaBGP then writing and reading to standard in and out is redirected straight to ExaBGP. This means that if you call print or stdout.write in your .py script then the output should not go to the console.

That being said you can use a stderr output from the .py script to output things to the console. You can disable any exaBGP output by simply setting enable = false (from the env file you have provided above). This will only show what you have outputted to stderr out from your .py script.

If you want to keep the exaBGP output as well you can also redirect it to a file, if that helps. Here is a extract from the Ubuntu manpage which describes the env attribute to do this:

```
exabgp.log.destination: Controls where logging should be sent. 
                           syslog (or no setting) sends the data to the local syslog
                           server.  host:<location> sends the data to a remote
                           syslog server.  stdout sends the data to stdout.
                           stderr sends the data to stderr.  <filename> sends
                           the data to the named file.  Default: stdout.
```

Just be careful if you are trying to pipe the output from exaBGP to another app as your .py script console output will be redirected to stderr not stdout.

You could also write a quick log function in your .py script that writes the output to a file. This however will depend on what you are using the output for.

Hopefully that helps. Also here is a link to the Ubuntu manpage for ExaBGP that contains some of the env variables supported and descriptions about them (just in case you are unsure what some do).

[http://manpages.ubuntu.com/manpages/wily/man1/exabgp.1.html](http://manpages.ubuntu.com/manpages/wily/man1/exabgp.1.html)

Two wei:

-----

> some kinds of disconnects suggest that we should not try to reconnect to it later (is peer myself? is it replying with protocol-breaking headers? etc).

A common case on the ETH public mainnet is other-genesis peers with the same network ID that otherwise follow all [sub-]protocol rules correctly.

This can get annoying/overwhelming, especially since it consumes precious resources. `geth`/`parity` APIs aren't very comprehensive on this, so in a few cases I've resorted to firewall-level connection filtering (think `iptables`/`shorewall`/`fail2ban`).

-----

> Add a peer blacklist

May I suggest more generic/neutral language?.. Like: blocklist; but really: tier, filter, sieve.

The reason is that these things tend to get layered. A not entirely fictional scenario:

* first I want to just block the irrelevant;
* but then I want a limited set of "priority" peers (e.g. a VPN ring specified in a text file);
* then there happens to be "customers" that maybe I'd drop on a rainy day, but they're paying, so I try not to;
* eventually, there's the "too slow" category that gets dropped during low load periods, and when I myself get dropped for the same reason during high load, this is the pool I'd like to dip into, instead of thrashing around randomly.

I guess the gist is: all of these involve peer management, are application-specific and can't be guessed by developers in advance.

A blacklist/blocklist is fine as a start, but long-term this is best given away to the node operator's middleware.

So the idea is that a transaction that came from us locally should be treated slightly differently than one which we received from another peer.  We want these to be persisted reliably across trinity restarts and to be validated using looser validation rules.

Here are some ways that I think local and remote transactions need to be treated separately.

- validity:
  - local: any transaction which passes strict objective validation stays
  - remote: we may discard transactions with low gas prices or which we find another transaction from the same sender which is still pending but with a higher nonce
- persistence
  - local: high reliability that a node restart will not lose the transaction
  - remote: we may discard transactions that have not yet been included in the chain for some reasons
- priority
  - local: should take precedence
  - remote: lower precedence

> A pending transaction was one that is in a block which has not been mined/finalized yes?

Partially: in some cases they might not be in any block at all. Two kinds of relevant pending transactions that follow slightly different rules:

- _peer-received_: This populates the txpool. Can be dropped across restarts, or by failing arbitrary (trinity-chosen) tests about gas price, etc.
- _locally-issued_: A user requested to broadcast this transaction (eg~ via `eth_sendRawTransaction'). We likely want to persist these across trinity restarts, and want to keep these as pending regardless of the txpool garbage collection rules related to gas price, etc.

> I think if so then I support removing these specialized getters assuming we can do what we need without them.

Because they might not be in any block, I think we need to keep them.

> Maybe they were for looking up transactions by hash when they had not yet been included in a canonical block?

Right, it's valid to make a json-rpc call requesting the body of a pending transaction. Even if we punt for a while on doing this from the transaction pool, doing this for locally issued transactions early seems important.

> so maybe there's no need for any pushback?

I think of the proliferation of similar standards as a kind of [Public Bad](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Public_bad), which forces work on all the software that supports it. The cost isn't that high for us, but there are global costs to having yet another type. The global benefit/cost seem poor in this case. But fighting the proliferation is personally high-cost and very-low-benefit, so whether it's worth it is a personal decision of how charitable I'm feeling. :) One potential benefit of pushing back is that it helps me learn the subtleties of undocumented differences.
I'll get this merged and released when tests are green.

FYI, there is a larger separate issue that I don't think has been discussed and I don't think we currently handle.

When parsing log data, we *assume* that because the first topic matches the topic derived from the event signature, that it will be well-formed for decoding via the ABI for the given event.  This assumption is easily broken. As for the nature of the error you're receiving. 
A 400 error indicates that the `body` your sending to ES is malformed. It is expecting the body to be in the form of 
```json
{
    "doc":{},
    "script":{}
}
```

You can see what the ES API is expecting when doing an update by looking at the docs here: https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/reference/current/docs-update.html#_updates_with_a_partial_document

I think what you need to do is in your `if` statement, you should format the `body` so that it conforms to the `body` that the ES API is expecting.

Something that I don't like about the current proposal after applying two comments regarding your concerns::

1) Making the `trace_context` an arg instead of a `kwarg` breaks many function signatures such as `connect`, `direct_connect` and so on. And also IMHO makes the API of at least the `Connector` and its derivates dirtier. The same will happen with other classes that are used by the `ClientSession` and have to use signals to trace a specific event related to a specific request.
2) Removing the signals from the `Connector` and putting them together into the `ClientSession` forces us to add another param, the session, that will even make dirtier these functions. The same will happen with other classes.

To fix the second issue, trying to minimize the number of parameters needed I will be keen on creating an object that stores all of the dependencies needed to send a signal, perhaps::

```python
class Trace:
    def __init__(self, session, trace_context):
        self._session = session
        self._trace_context = trace_context

    def on_connect_start(self, host):
        self._session.on_connect_start.send(self._trace_context, host)

def connect(self, host, trace):
    trace.on_connect_start.send(host)
    trace.on_connect_start.send(host)
```

The first issue, that was other the concerns that you expressed @asvetlov, related to make the `trace` argument mandatory, I will be still more inclined to make them optional having, as a result, the following snippet::

```python
def connect(self, host, trace=None):
    if trace:
        trace.on_connect_start.send(host)
    if trace:
        trace.on_connect_start.send(host)
```

The `Trace` function won't be a first citizen class that could be created per each request in the `ClientSession._request` function:

```pyhton
def _request(self, ..)
    trace = Trace(self, trace_context)
    self.connector.connect(trace=trace)
```

I'm still wondering if it's the best implementation have the signals attached to `ClientSession`, and if its worth it to move them to another object called `TraceRequest`, passing this object  to the `ClientSession` at constructor time.  perhaps::

```python
trace_request = TraceRequest()
trace_request.on_request_start.append(my_function)
session = ClientSession(..., trace_request=trace_request)
```

When the developer wants to trace a specific client session gives it as a parameter, meanwhile the default behavior doesn't trace the requests. It will allow us to use the `freeze` method of the signals, once the `TraceRequest` instance is given to `ClientSession` all signals belonging to that specific `TraceRequest` instance are frozen.

Worth mentioning that having the signals to trace a request decoupled from the session, it forces us to pass the session instance as the first parameter for all of the signals.

Thoughts ?

Perhaps when webroot-path is set via the config file, [WebrootPathProcessor](https://github.com/letsencrypt/letsencrypt/pull/1594/files#diff-54b2f468d57ef7436008bd031bc89711R1065) isn't called?  Or when the fields come from the config file, some of the ordering assumptions that underpin [these changes](https://github.com/letsencrypt/letsencrypt/pull/1594/files) might be violated?

In the mean time, you can set `webroot-map` in your config file.  It has to look like this IIRC:
```
webroot-map = {"domain.com,www.domain.com" : "/var/www/domain", "eg.is" : "/var/www/is"}
```
Unfortunately, you can't have newlines in there ATM because ConfigArgParse can't cope with them.  For now domains must be specified in addition to webroot-map, though in the future the latter may imply the former.

You need to upgrade to confluent-kafka-python v0.9.4

And that's your culprit: `acks` specifies the number of required acks a producer must receive from the broker for deeming the message produced.
When you give it 0 the producer wont have to way for any ack from the broker so as soon as the producer has sent the messages they are deemed succesful.
In your case with the immediate destruction of the producer it will close the socket immediately and purge any messages waiting in the kernel socket buffer to be sent to the broker.
The faster your hardware is (or slower the network), the bigger risk this is of happening.

You should generally not use acks: 0 unless you are fine with message loss,
 you should at least go for acks=1 (acked by leader broker only), or if you want each message to be replicated to in-sync replicas use acks=all.

Hi, I’m Philipp – also from Fabian Theis’ lab – and I designed AnnData with Alex.

I wrote [a little piece](https://github.com/theislab/anndata/issues/2#issuecomment-346377365) on why we need to stop talking about “row annotation” and “column annotation”. There’s only cells and genes, rows and columns are exclusively an implementation detail
Because of this, here’s the vital bit about to “how to transpose our matrix”:

> We use genes as columns as column access is faster and we query for individual genes more often than columns

In sparse data more than dense, there’s two major storage types, [CSR](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sparse_matrix#Compressed_sparse_row_.28CSR.2C_CRS_or_Yale_format.29) and [CSC](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sparse_matrix#Compressed_sparse_column_.28CSC_or_CCS.29). I think in dense data, the almost-universal convention is column-major (columns are continuous memory sections and therefore faster to access).

Now there’s two considerations:

1. reading/writing speed and storage size. there’s a [difference for sparse data](https://github.com/vatlab/VarStore/issues/13).
2. algorithm speed. I assume e.g. kNN graph creation works better with cells in the continuous memory dimension, while @pkharchenko said that they do many per-gene tests, preferring genes to be there.

I propose:

1. We check which layout has the better size and read/write speed for high #cells and lower #genes.
2. We do a real-world benchmark of multiple workflows, once with genes in the continuous memory dimension and once with cells, and look at the results.

Only then can we make an informed decision what to choose.

The `#` symbol isn't used for comments in RiveScript, replace them with `//` if you want comments in your code.

I updated your code to this:

```
+ *               // generic reply
- I don't know your words, or speak loudly, please

+ a
- the letter A

+ b
- the letter B

+ what is letter *
- {@<star>}                 // works nice with A and B, But what if I ask for C ?!!!

// +  ????????  What could be the trigger, for this reply on unknown letters ???????
// - I don't know this letter !
```

And this gets this result:

```
You> hi
Bot> I don't know your words, or speak loudly, please
You> a
Bot> the letter A
You> b
Bot> the letter B
You> c
Bot> I don't know your words, or speak loudly, please
You> what is letter A  
Bot> the letter A
You> what is letter b
Bot> the letter B
You> what is letter C
Bot> I don't know your words, or speak loudly, please
```

Pro tip: if you turn on debug mode (e.g. by `python shell.py --debug` or using `debug=True` in the constructor) you can see what RiveScript is doing. With your original code, when I said "what is letter c" this is what it did under the hood:

```
You> what is letter c
[RS] Get reply to [localuser] what is letter c
[RS] Checking topic random for any %Previous's.
[RS] There is a %Previous in this topic!
[RS] lastReply: the letter a                  works nice with a and b but what if i ask for c 
[RS] Try to match 'what is letter c' against 'a' ('^a$')
[RS] Try to match 'what is letter c' against 'b' ('^b$')
[RS] Try to match 'what is letter c' against '*               # generic reply' ('^(.+?)               (\\d+?) generic reply$')
[RS] Try to match 'what is letter c' against 'what is letter *' ('^what is letter (.+?)$')
[RS] Found a match!
[RS] Reply: {@<star>}                 # works nice with A and B, But what if I ask for C ?!!!
[RS] Redirect to c
[RS] Try to match 'c' against 'a' ('^a$')
[RS] Try to match 'c' against 'b' ('^b$')
[RS] Try to match 'c' against '*               # generic reply' ('^(.+?)               (\\d+?) generic reply$')
[RS] Try to match 'c' against 'what is letter *' ('^what is letter (.+?)$')
Bot> [ERR: No reply matched]
```

The second to last thing it tried to match against was your `*` trigger, which had the wrong `#` symbol for comments and you can see why it wouldn't match the letter "c".

I have a naming question: Do we prefix everything with `JSONAPI` or override existing names in DRF? We now have both styles:
- DJA serializers override DRF's `HyperlinkedModelSerializer` and so on. The advantage is code written for DRF can upgrade to DJA by simply changing an import. The disadvantage is that it's easy to "forget" to change the import from `rest_framework` to `rest_framework_json_api`.
- DJA paginators are named `JSONAPIPageNumberPagination` instead of overriding DRF's `PageNumberPagination`. The advantage is that it's very obvious that this is DJA-specific. The disadvantage is that instead of changing one import, you have to change each class use instance.

The approach here could be either:
- [ ] DJA `DefaultRouter` overriding DRF `DefaultRouter`. 
- [ ] DJA `JSONAPIDefaultRouter`

> Yes, I agree if there is only one build system and if everybody is familiar with it, but those assumption don't hold here. Whether this is "completely standard" in other projects is of no relevance, Z3 is "not standard" in many respects, and yes, we can try make it look "more standard", but that will be a major effort and until we decide to actually go for something like that, I'd rather not disrupt and confuse existing operations and users. So, if it's little bit more effort to make it self-contained and non-disruptive then I think we should go for that.

I don't think the idea of splitting your projects into components and having each component depend on each other is non-standard. But that is exactly how Z3 is organised and what the CMake build system I've created is designed around.

As for a "little bit more effort".... it's not a little bit of effort. It would be a huge amount of effort with no real benefit. CMake is designed to be used in a particular way (the way I'm using it) and what you're suggesting is not a good fit for how CMake works. Also I am not willing to waste my time compromising the readability and maintainability of the CMake build system. I would much rather use my time doing something useful like teaching the CMake build system how to build and install Z3's language bindings which would bring the CMake build system to full feature parity with the Python build system.

Also the Python and CMake build systems can co-exist fairly painlessly. If you're modifying source files you probably won't need to modify the CMake build system. You would only need to modify it if you're adding a new ``.cpp`` file, dependency or build flags or something like that. From what I've observed of Z3's development over the last few months that doesn't seem to happen that often. Even so adding a new source file or component dependency requires only a tiny change to the relevant ``CMakeLists.txt`` file.

There's no tests explicitly doing this as it should only ever be done in the context of creating new objects or loading them from XML. In principle, the registry of an object has two purposes:

1. Have it globally available so the user can get access to it
2. Having the code know where to flush it to or fully load it from

Having an object in more than one registry makes no sense and if it were attempted, a copy should be made (and in fact *is* for the box).

There are only four lines of code in the codebase which actually set the registry to something and one of them is setting it to `None` on deletion and another is setting the metadata registry. Of the remaining two, one of them is just unnecessary repetition. In summary, there is only one real place where `_setRegistry` is called and that is via `Registry._add()`. There are in turn only four places that `Registry._add()` is called and they are all in sensible places.

I know we mark Job objects as not dirty after a flush but I don't think we correctly address marking all objects within the schema as clean afterwards, just the parent. Can we improve the ```_setFlushed``` method so we do things in future like what attributes are triggering most objects to become dirty.

@jlazarow:

> it seems a bit crazy to have to do that for every small complaint

I hear you, and I agree that forking the whole dashboard to add label information is not a long-term solution.

Like I mentioned, the problem is really in getting the label information from your TensorFlow session to the outside world. I’ve continued to think about it, and it seems like we might do the following. Currently, the image summary emits a rank-1 string tensor of the form `[width_str, height_str, png_0, png_1, …]`, where the `png_i` are bytestrings with PNG-encoded image data and `width_str` and `height_str` are ASCII representations of the image width and height in decimal (e.g., `"123"`). We could backward-compatibly allow the operation to instead output a rank-2 string tensor of the form `[[width_str, height_str, png_0, png_1, …], [ignored, ignored, markdown_label_0, markdown_label_1, …]]`, where `[markdown_label_0, markdown_label_2, …]` is a string tensor provided to the summary. I’ll see what other people on the team think about this, and then we can consider implementing it.

> I guess I'm trying to use TensorBoard from an experimental/research aspect (perhaps wrongly?)

Not wrongly at all—our primary goal is to help researchers write visualizations tailored to their research.

> The issue is that the interface between TF and TB is just too rigid

It is. In particular, the requirement that all summary output be a tensor, not something more struct-like, is very restrictive. (Things like "the images plugin emits a tensor the first two values of which are the stringified versions of the image dimensions" are already too hacky for my tastes…)

> It's already a hack to just change the names of summaries without introducing a new op

Happily, our new APIs support `display_name` and also `summary_description` parameters. The images demo (`//tensorboard/plugins/image:images_demo`) includes code that demonstrates how to use these parameters. You can see example screenshots in this PR: https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorboard/pull/314. Hope this helps a bit. :-)

`get_wm_name` defaults to using the `STRING` type. There are 3 string types: `STRING`, `UTF8_STRING`, and `COMPOUND_TEXT`. Depending on how the name was set, you could try:
```python
window.get_full_text_property(Xatom.WM_NAME, display.get_atom('UTF8_STRING'))
```
This should return a proper Unicode string if the property was set with `UTF8_STRING`. `xterm -title`, for example, will use `COMPOUND_TEXT`, which is unsupported: you'll get the raw bytes, not a decoded Unicode string (more info [here](https://github.com/python-xlib/python-xlib/pull/52#issue-79680611)).

```Python

Something else to think about: would it make more sense applying this do-no-track attribute to the type, rather than the return value?  Otherwise I could get into weird behaviour with something like:

```Python++
p = best(p1, p2)
```
where `best` is some sort of max-like function that returns a reference to the better instance.  Suppose it returns a reference to `p1`.  I'm going to get different behaviour depending on whether `p1` came from a tracked or untracked return value.  If `p1` was tracked, I'll get `p is p1`.  If untracked, what happens?  I *think* I would get two variables that Python thinks are different, but that are bound to the same C++ instance; is this going to work properly?

If, on the other hand, the don't-track-me is a class property, I'll always get consistent behaviour: *every* function that returns a `Foo` (in your example) will be returning untracked copies.  (In the `best` call above, I'd always get a `p` copied from `p1` and `p2`, regardless of where `p1` or `p2` came from).

The core issue in #1138 is that we don't check the holder type at all when initializing an instance: we just assume that a function returning a holder is returning the right kind of holder.  I've just submitted #1161 to detect that and fail if it happens when attempting to register the type and/or function.

There are some other cases this PR doesn't catch that the other one does: for example returning a `std::shared_ptr<A>` that needs a `std::unique_ptr<A>`, or one that returns a `boost::shared_ptr<A>` to a type needing an `std::shared_ptr<A>`, or various other combinations.

More problematic, the change here would also break backwards-compatibility (and indeed breaks the current test suite) by requiring that any move-only holder adds a `release()` method.

Conversion between holder types--e.g. being able to convert an rvalue `unique_ptr` into a `shared_ptr` seems feasible, but I think it's going to need a different path than stealing the pointer and stashing it in the new type.  `std::shared_ptr` *has* a `std::unique_ptr &&` constructor for doing exactly this promotion, but we'd need a nice way to detect that case, and ideally, detect it at compile time rather than run-time.

pybind11 splits memory allocation into an ``operator new`` (https://github.com/pybind/pybind11/blob/master/include/pybind11/class_support.h#L186) and followed by a call to the placement new operator. The former part is non-templated/generic and does not know about alignment, hence the assertion failure you're seeing.

C++17 has a nice alignent-aware ``operator new``, but alas, C++17 is not widely supported.

Unfortunately, converting between a public PEM and a raw key can involve more than just stripping data. Because encryption, there are several different formats that keys can be stored in. Just clipping out the last 64 octets off the key is usually "OK" in the same way that using duct tape to fix your rear bumper is "OK". It's really far better to decode the PEM using ASN1 to get the values, but that's overkill for now.

That said, there are a few things a play here that may be tripping you up. Chrome's WebPush requires a few more steps than Firefox does, and has a different auth mechanism. 

In short you don't use VAPID with Chrome's WebPush, you use FCM.
https://github.com/GoogleChrome/samples/tree/gh-pages/push-messaging-and-notifications
provides an example of how to send messages via Chrome's Webpush/FCM 

So, for your example, you'd need to create a `manifest.json` file including the `"gcm_sender_id"` that matches whatever your Chrome FCM project's `Sender ID` is, and then send the notification with 
```
curl --header "Authorization: key=_YourLongFCMServerKey_" \
--header "Content-Type: application/json" \
-d "{\"registration_ids\":[\"_RegistrationIds_\"]}"
```
The _RegistrationIds_ are provided from [the last segment of the endpointURL](https://github.com/GoogleChrome/samples/blob/gh-pages/push-messaging-and-notifications/main.js#L58) (Probably should note the extra conditions for older versions of Chrome)

I've tried out the code sample above and have been able to send a message through to Chrome. I'm a bit sad that they're taking this approach rather than VAPID, but then VAPID isn't yet formalized as a standard and FCM already works, so I'm not going to ding them about it. 

Now, as for the 401/109 you're seeing for firefox, I'm not sure I know why that failed. It may be that you're not properly encoding the public key (again, just stripping off octets may not do what you think).

Glad to hear it, and thanks for trying it out! Sounds like there's a bug in the local scheduler (perhaps similar to the previous bug).

You're rebuilding all of Ray, right? Because the local scheduler also communicates with the plasma store, so it probably needs the same fix from apache/arrow#1172.

Some processes log to `/tmp/raylogs`, so it's worth looking at the most recent files in there and see if anything turns up, but if you're starting Ray with `ray.init()`, then the local scheduler STDERR/STDOUT will just go to the terminal.

What I would suggest is trying to run the same workload that is causing the crash, but to start the local scheduler in gdb. To do that, you could do something like the following.

1. First modify

https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/aebe9f937451bfa10aa0f2a41bafcf4747fb60f0/python/ray/local_scheduler/local_scheduler_services.py#L122

to be something like

```python
import IPython
IPython.embed()
# pid = subprocess.Popen(command, stdout=stdout_file, stderr=stderr_file)
pid = 9999
```

2. Then start Python and do `import ray` and `ray.init()`. This will open up IPython when it tries to start the local scheduler. Run `print(command)` in the IPython shell to print the command that Ray wants to use to start the local scheduler.

3. Then go to a different terminal window, and do

```
gdb ray/python/ray/core/src/local_scheduler/local_scheduler
```

Then do `run ` followed by the command printed by `print(command)`. to start the local scheduler in gdb. However, you'll need to drop the initial executable from the command, AND you'll need to add quotes around the full argument to the `-w` flag, which is pretty long. Otherwise you'll get an error saying unknown flag or something like that.

4. Then go back to the IPython shell and do `exit()`

5. Then run your workload and see what errors are caught in gdb.

Note that if the error is uninformative, we may need to recompile Ray with more debug information. E.g., maybe add a `-g` to the line

https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/aebe9f937451bfa10aa0f2a41bafcf4747fb60f0/src/common/CMakeLists.txt#L9
