> For example, each time you're faced with the decision of whether to pass the `*args` in the `partial()` call, or pass only the `**kwargs` and leave the `*args` for `run()`. The latter spreads the arguments across more than one invocation, which isn't ideal for code comprehension.

I guess my convention is to always use either `run(fn, arg1, arg2, run_arg=...)` or `run(partial(fn, arg1, arg2, kwarg=something), run_arg=...)`. I agree that `run(partial(fn, kwarg=something), arg1, arg2)` would be very weird style.

> And if the former should be used, then why not insist that `partial()` always be used? That way there'd be only "one way."

Yeah, it really comes down to taste. On balance I think the advantages of being able to write `run(fn, arg1, arg2)` are enough to make it worthwhile, but if you'd rather write `run(partial(fn, arg1, arg2))` then go for it I guess :-).

> (Actually, some parts of `asyncio`'s API do require partial, like `Future.add_done_callback()`.)

¯\\\_(ツ)\_/¯

> It would be some marriage of a tuple and a dict and allow passing `*args` and `**kwargs` to a function using a single value (e.g. `func(***pkargs)` or a similar syntax). Do you know if something like this has ever been proposed?

Huh, no, I haven't seen that idea before.

You can't emit a compile time warning about first class object references, as if you do, they're not first class anymore. From a Python compiler's point of view, `f.close` is no different from `f.name`, just as `make_coroutine`, `make_generator`, `make_function`, and `make_value` are all opaque identifiers.

Because of the REPL and try-except statements, you can't even issue a `Warning, expression statement has no effect` for non-call expression statements, since you may just be referencing an object to get the `repr()` of it (and the implicit assignment to `_`), or perhaps looking for a `NameError`, `AttributeError`, `KeyError`, or `IndexError`.

You *can* create languages which don't have first class runtime functions/classes/methods/etc, but then you need to introduce a special metasyntax for higher order metaprogramming, rather than have it be so natural that people may not even realise they're doing it (e.g. `@classmethod`, `self.addCleanup(f.close)`, or `sorted(seq, key=tuple)`.

You can potentially put more warnings into tools like `mypy`, since their control flow analysis can pipe up and say "this looks weird", and you're not typically going to be using them in a REPL.

But at compile time itself? No - Python deliberately defers too much to runtime to allow for that. (Even the "Missing parentheses in call to print" hack is implemented in the SyntaxError constructor for cases that are already Py3 syntax errors - it doesn't catch the bare `print` case)

Looking at this again, I'm not as certain it's a good idea as I (apparently) was when I created it, so I'm switching the labels :-).

To clarify what the proposal actually is, though: normally a nursery is closed when all the tasks inside it exit. Say I have a nursery. I hand it off to some other code, which will stash it somewhere, and eventually – at some unknown point in the future – spawn tasks into it. This creates a possible race condition: the nursery was open at the time I handed it off, but what if it closes between when I hand it off and when the receiving code actually starts using it? If the receiving code could immediately mark the nursery as "in use", then it could solve the race condition. And we already have essentially this functionality, internally, via the `Nursery._pending_starts` attribute – the `Nursery.start` method uses it to make sure that if the nursery is open at the time `start` is called, then it will still be open when the task calls `task_status.started()`.

I'm having some trouble imagining exactly when this would be useful though. It's pretty unusual for someone to open a nursery in one task, pass it to a sibling, etc. Maybe it's really important, but I'd like it better if I had a clear example of a situation where all the pieces lined up to make it useful.

I think current semantics are well-defined - i.e. for conv/pool/etc one applies `out_width = floor((in_width + pad_left + pad_right - kernel_width) / stride_width) + 1` with shift to the left/top.

The reason to go this way instead of enforcing perfect divisibility you described are following:
1) there can be models with varying input size (e.g. superresolution or style transfer). In that case it's impossible to get your formula correct statically
2) majority of frameworks are actually using this approach of not enforcing divisibility and prefer symmetric padding overall
3) we don't have to put additional burden on frontends to do the extra pass and figure out whether the gap is on the left


For the caffe pooling/conv discrepancy it was actually a bug/feature in Caffe :) I'm not aware of other frameworks following this logic. We had a discussion about it earlier and decided to deprecate this behavior. If you want to have historical perspective here's a doc in Caffe2: https://github.com/caffe2/caffe2/blob/master/caffe2/proto/caffe2_legacy.proto#L23 

Indeed in Caffe case one needs to figure out correct padding based on the input size. But it's better to get rid of the legacy behavior instead of carrying it over forever.

This subject could become quite interesting but the way it's been asked it is quite lazy without any relevant code/details/information at all. Let's try to make this post a little bit more interesting with some related questions:

0) Is it a possible using EBNF grammar to parse natural language words like persian or it's better using more suitable tools like [ntlk](https://www.nltk.org/)?
1) Can EBNF grammars cover natural languages like persian/english/...? EBNF accepts grammars type 2, that means neither context-sensitive grammar nor unrestricted grammars can be parsed by it...
2) Let's consider the simplistic assumption than a valid `persian string` is just 1 or more persian alphabets letters.

The above snippet will raise `lark.lexer.UnexpectedInput: No token defined for: 'ر' in 'رشته ' at line 1 col 0`. So, how do you recognize unicode letters on lark grammars?

Update: there is a work-around to freeze the convolutional layers in the default model.
In calamari_ocr/ocr/backends/tensorflow_backend/tensorflow_model.py file, simply add argument **trainable= False** to **create_network()** function.

`layers.append(tf.layers.conv2d(
                            ...,
                            trainable=False,
                   )`
